title: Emergence of Open Chemical Reaction Network Thermodynamics within Closed Systems
summary: We address a fundamental question: under which conditions do the dynamics and thermodynamics of open chemical reaction networks (CRNs), grounded on the notion of idealized chemostats that exchange selected species, emerge from underlying closed CRNs? While open CRNs provide the standard framework to describe out-of-equilibrium chemical systems, real systems are finite and ultimately relax to equilibrium, leaving the status of this description conceptually unresolved. Here we show that open-CRN behavior arises as an asymptotic regime of closed CRNs when two minimal and physically transparent conditions are met: a time-scale separation, whereby fast reactions effectively act as exchange mechanisms, and an abundance separation, whereby a subset of species behaves as chemostats with diverging chemical capacity. In this regime, both the stochastic dynamics and the thermodynamic structure \ -- including local detailed balance, entropy production, and free-energy balance \ -- emerge to leading order from the underlying closed CRN. Our results apply to arbitrary stoichiometries. They show that chemostats need not be introduced as external idealizations, but instead arise as emergent thermodynamic structures within closed systems, providing a unified and physically grounded foundation for the nonequilibrium thermodynamics of CRNs.

AI.tldr: Open chemical reaction networks (CRNs) emerge as asymptotic regimes from closed CRNs when time-scale and abundance separations occur, providing a unified foundation for nonequilibrium thermodynamics.
AI.motivation: To resolve the conceptual gap between idealized open CRNs (standard framework for out-of-equilibrium systems) and real closed systems that ultimately relax to equilibrium, by showing how open-CRN behavior emerges from underlying closed CRNs.
AI.method: Demonstrate that open-CRN behavior arises as an asymptotic regime of closed CRNs when two minimal conditions are met: (1) time-scale separation where fast reactions act as exchange mechanisms, and (2) abundance separation where certain species behave as chemostats with diverging chemical capacity.
AI.result: In this emergent regime, both stochastic dynamics and thermodynamic structure (local detailed balance, entropy production, free-energy balance) emerge to leading order from the underlying closed CRN, applicable to arbitrary stoichiometries.
AI.conclusion: Chemostats need not be introduced as external idealizations but arise as emergent thermodynamic structures within closed systems, providing a unified and physically grounded foundation for nonequilibrium thermodynamics of CRNs.

title: Coarse Graining Reveals a Fluctuation-theorem-like Asymmetry in Financial Markets
summary: Fluctuation theorems show how coarse graining transforms microscopic symmetry into observable irreversibility. Here we ask whether an analogous symmetrybased diagnostic can be constructed for financial markets. At the microscopic level, each transaction pairs a buyer and a seller, whereas trading decisions are typically made from coarse-grained price histories. Using symmetric takeprofit and stop-loss rules, we compare the holding-time distributions of long and short trading ensembles generated from the same price series. Across equityindices, individual stocks and cryptocurrencies, the log-ratio of the two distributions shows a robust crossover. It remains nearly constant at short durations but becomes linear in the tail, implying an exponential directional asymmetry. The tail slope defines an effective market temperature, an operational measure of fluctuation intensity on the chosen observation scale. A Bachelier first-passage benchmark captures the exponential tails but not the asymmetry, because long and short positions share the same leading decay rate. By contrast, short-time correlations between overlapping positions provide a minimal mechanism for the asymmetry by generating direction-dependent subleading relaxation spectra in a coarse-grained Markov description. Together, these results establish a fluctuation-theorem-like diagnostic of irreversibility in financial markets and, more broadly, in complex systems accessible only through coarse-grained observables.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a fluctuation-theorem-like diagnostic for financial market irreversibility by analyzing holding-time distributions of symmetric long/short trading strategies across various assets.
AI.motivation: To construct a symmetry-based diagnostic for financial markets analogous to fluctuation theorems in statistical physics, which transform microscopic symmetry into observable irreversibility through coarse graining.
AI.method: Use symmetric take-profit and stop-loss rules to generate long and short trading ensembles from the same price series. Compare holding-time distributions of these ensembles across equity indices, individual stocks, and cryptocurrencies. Analyze the log-ratio of distributions to detect directional asymmetry.
AI.result: The log-ratio shows a robust crossover: nearly constant at short durations but linear in the tail, implying exponential directional asymmetry. The tail slope defines an effective market temperature. A Bachelier first-passage benchmark captures exponential tails but not the asymmetry, while short-time correlations between overlapping positions provide a minimal mechanism for the asymmetry.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a fluctuation-theorem-like diagnostic of irreversibility in financial markets and complex systems accessible only through coarse-grained observables, with directional asymmetry arising from subleading relaxation spectra in a coarse-grained Markov description.

title: Thermodynamic Geometry of Relaxation
comment: 6 pages(2 figures)+13 pages Supplemental Materials; Comments are welcome!
summary: While the geometry of equilibrium states and driven non-equilibrium processes is clearly understood, a geometric description for relaxation towards equilibrium is still lacking. Here, we propose a thermo-geometric measure based on the Rayleigh quotient, reformulating relaxation as a fundamental competition between entropic stiffness and frictional dissipation. Taking a van der Waals gas with two dissipation channels as an example, we explicitly demonstrate its relaxation landscape. Particularly, we find that upon approaching the critical temperature $T_c$, the slow-mode relaxation rate vanishes linearly as $λ_s \propto (T-T_c)/T_c$, indicating critical slowing down. This study completes the thermodynamic geometry framework, providing a general tool for characterizing the relaxation dynamics of complex systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a thermo-geometric measure using Rayleigh quotient to describe relaxation toward equilibrium, completing thermodynamic geometry framework.
AI.motivation: While equilibrium states and driven non-equilibrium processes have clear geometric descriptions, a geometric framework for relaxation toward equilibrium is still lacking.
AI.method: Develops a thermo-geometric measure based on the Rayleigh quotient, reformulating relaxation as competition between entropic stiffness and frictional dissipation. Demonstrates using van der Waals gas with two dissipation channels.
AI.result: Explicitly demonstrates relaxation landscape for van der Waals gas. Finds that near critical temperature Tc, slow-mode relaxation rate vanishes linearly as λs ∝ (T-Tc)/Tc, indicating critical slowing down.
AI.conclusion: Completes the thermodynamic geometry framework, providing a general tool for characterizing relaxation dynamics of complex systems.

title: Scrambling of Entanglement from Integrability to Chaos: Bootstrapped Time-Integrated Spread Complexity
comment: 6 pages, 4 figures, initial public release, PACS: 05.45.Mt, 03.67.Lx, 81Qxx, 02.60.C
summary: A time-integrated measure of complexity is proposed for diagnosing the degree of quantum ergodicity. The scrambling dynamics of maximally entangled states within the ensemble of unitary evolutions are quantified by applying numerical bootstrapped realizations of an ensemble of Hamiltonians, probing different unitary paths. Using Rosenzweig-Porter ensembles, we show that the integrated spread complexity provides a fine-grained resolution across different ergodic regimes. This approach offers a robust method for diagnosing quantum chaos from early to late times.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a time-integrated measure of complexity for diagnosing quantum ergodicity, using scrambling dynamics of maximally entangled states and numerical bootstrapping of Hamiltonian ensembles.
AI.motivation: To develop a robust method for diagnosing quantum chaos across different ergodic regimes, from early to late times, using complexity measures.
AI.method: Proposes time-integrated spread complexity measure; applies numerical bootstrapped realizations of Hamiltonian ensembles; quantifies scrambling dynamics of maximally entangled states within unitary evolution ensembles; uses Rosenzweig-Porter ensembles for testing.
AI.result: Integrated spread complexity provides fine-grained resolution across different ergodic regimes; offers robust diagnostic method for quantum chaos throughout time evolution.
AI.conclusion: The proposed time-integrated complexity measure effectively diagnoses quantum ergodicity and provides a robust tool for analyzing quantum chaos across different time scales and ergodic regimes.

title: Magnitude Is All You Need? Rethinking Phase in Quantum Encoding of Complex SAR Data
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures. Under review for IEEE QCE 2026
summary:
Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) data is inherently complex-valued, while quantum machine learning (QML) models naturally operate in complex Hilbert spaces. This apparent alignment suggests that incorporating both magnitude and phase information into quantum encoding should improve performance in SAR Automatic Target Recognition (ATR). In this work, we systematically evaluate this assumption by comparing five quantum encoding strategies: magnitude-only, joint complex, I/Q-based, preprocessed phase, and pure quantum, under a unified experimental framework on the MSTAR benchmark dataset.
  Contrary to expectation, we observe a consistent pattern: in hybrid quantum-classical architectures, magnitude-only encoding outperforms all complex-valued strategies, achieving 99.57% accuracy on a 3-class task and 71.19% on an 8-class task, while phase-aware methods provide negligible (~0%) or negative improvements. In contrast, in purely quantum architectures with only 184-224 trainable parameters and no classical components, phase information becomes essential, contributing up to 21.65% improvement in accuracy.
  These results reveal that the utility of phase information is not inherent to the data, but depends critically on the model architecture. Hybrid models rely on classical components that compensate for missing phase information, whereas purely quantum models require phase to construct discriminative representations. Our findings provide practical design guidelines for encoding complex-valued data in QML and highlight the importance of encoding-architecture co-design in the NISQ era.

AI.tldr: Quantum encoding strategies for SAR ATR show magnitude-only encoding outperforms complex-valued methods in hybrid quantum-classical models, while phase information becomes essential in purely quantum architectures.
AI.motivation: SAR data is complex-valued and QML operates in complex Hilbert spaces, suggesting that incorporating both magnitude and phase information into quantum encoding should improve SAR ATR performance.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of five quantum encoding strategies (magnitude-only, joint complex, I/Q-based, preprocessed phase, and pure quantum) under unified experimental framework on MSTAR benchmark dataset, comparing hybrid quantum-classical vs. purely quantum architectures.
AI.result: Magnitude-only encoding outperforms all complex-valued strategies in hybrid architectures (99.57% accuracy on 3-class, 71.19% on 8-class), while phase-aware methods provide negligible or negative improvements. In purely quantum architectures (184-224 parameters), phase information becomes essential, contributing up to 21.65% accuracy improvement.
AI.conclusion: Utility of phase information depends critically on model architecture, not inherent data properties. Hybrid models compensate for missing phase via classical components, while purely quantum models require phase for discriminative representations. Findings provide practical QML encoding guidelines and highlight encoding-architecture co-design importance in NISQ era.

title: Revealing the physical structure of the general quantum master equation
summary: The Lindblad (GKLS) master equation, which represents the mathematical form for the general evolution of a density matrix, is a versatile and widely-used tool in open quantum systems. In contrast with the typical approach of imposing additional conditions on the system, such as weak coupling or energy conservation, we explore the structure of the equation with no assumptions. We demonstrate that general quantum dynamics can be expressed through a combination of free evolution, exchanges of some physical quantities (generalised charges), not necessarily commuting with the Hamiltonian, between the system and the bath, and pure dephasing. This result comprises a novel perspective on quantum master equations, employing physical processes as elemental parts. We use it to explore the dynamics and stationary states of a two-level system and show that strong coupling, particle exchange, and non-Abelian effects all share the same physical origin. Moreover, we demonstrate that the generalised Gibbs state for all three cases contains a non-commutation term, which has not been previously considered.

AI.tldr: The paper provides a novel perspective on Lindblad master equations by decomposing general quantum dynamics into free evolution, exchange of generalized charges, and pure dephasing, without requiring weak coupling or energy conservation assumptions.
AI.motivation: To move beyond typical restrictions in open quantum systems theory (like weak coupling or energy conservation) and provide a more fundamental understanding of Lindblad master equations by identifying the elemental physical processes underlying general quantum dynamics.
AI.method: Analyze the structure of the Lindblad (GKLS) master equation without additional assumptions, decomposing general quantum dynamics into three components: free evolution, exchange of generalized charges (which may not commute with the Hamiltonian), and pure dephasing.
AI.result: Demonstrated that strong coupling, particle exchange, and non-Abelian effects share the same physical origin in the exchange of generalized charges. Showed that the generalized Gibbs state for all three cases contains a previously overlooked non-commutation term, using a two-level system as an illustrative example.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a novel framework for understanding quantum master equations through physical processes rather than mathematical constraints, revealing fundamental connections between seemingly different phenomena and identifying missing terms in generalized Gibbs state descriptions.

title: Consciousness, Quantum Mechanics, and the Limits of Scientific Objectivism
summary: Consciousness and quantum mechanics are among the most puzzling phenomena studied in the sciences. Some scholars suggest they are related, though others think this claim commits a "minimization of mystery" fallacy. The aim of this programmatic paper is to draw attention to a less widely discussed parallel between consciousness and quantum mechanics: both challenge the classical objectivist worldview of science. Under certain assumptions, they are each in tension with a package of metaphysical theses -- "non-relationalism", "non-fragmentation", and "one world" -- that jointly make up that worldview. This points to three distinct non-objectivist responses: the "relationalist", "fragmentalist", and "many-subjective-worlds" ones. We will map out their pros and cons.

AI.tldr: The paper explores parallels between consciousness and quantum mechanics, arguing both challenge classical objectivist worldview through tensions with non-relationalism, non-fragmentation, and one-world theses, proposing three non-objectivist responses.
AI.motivation: To examine the less-discussed parallel between consciousness and quantum mechanics as both phenomena that challenge the classical objectivist scientific worldview, addressing the tension they create with fundamental metaphysical assumptions.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis mapping how consciousness and quantum mechanics each challenge three metaphysical theses (non-relationalism, non-fragmentation, one world), then evaluating three corresponding non-objectivist philosophical responses.
AI.result: Identifies three distinct non-objectivist responses to the challenges: relationalist, fragmentalist, and many-subjective-worlds approaches, each with specific pros and cons in addressing the tensions between consciousness/quantum mechanics and classical objectivism.
AI.conclusion: Both consciousness and quantum mechanics fundamentally challenge classical objectivist assumptions, requiring consideration of alternative metaphysical frameworks that accommodate relational, fragmented, or multiple-subjective perspectives on reality.

title: Quantum instanton approach to metastable collective spins
summary: Collective spin systems -- spin ensembles coupled to a common reservoir and effectively described by a single macrospin -- play an important role in both atomic and solid-state physics. Their intrinsic nonlinearity gives rise to multiple long-lived metastable states that ultimately relax to a unique most probable state. This dominant state can change with a control parameter, leading to first-order phase transitions. We develop a real-time instanton approach based on quantum quasiprobability dynamics that captures the stationary state in the large-spin limit and the asymptotic scaling of relaxation rates. We further show that these features are not accurately described by the previously applied semiclassical Wigner approach due to its neglect of non-Gaussian fluctuations.

AI.tldr: Developed real-time instanton approach for collective spin systems that captures stationary states and relaxation rates better than semiclassical Wigner method.
AI.motivation: Collective spin systems exhibit multiple metastable states and first-order phase transitions, but existing semiclassical Wigner approach fails to accurately describe these features due to neglect of non-Gaussian fluctuations.
AI.method: Developed a real-time instanton approach based on quantum quasiprobability dynamics that works in the large-spin limit.
AI.result: The approach captures stationary states and asymptotic scaling of relaxation rates, showing superior accuracy compared to semiclassical Wigner method.
AI.conclusion: Non-Gaussian fluctuations are crucial for accurately describing collective spin systems, and the new instanton approach provides a better theoretical framework than previous semiclassical methods.

title: Quantum correlation tests at cosmic distances
comment: Accepted in Annales de la Fondation Louis de Broglie
summary: It is commonly accepted that the results of measurements simultaneously realized over two entangled subsystems are statistically correlated instantaneously regardless of the distance between them. In accordance with Bell theorem, everything happens in such measurements as if there was a correlation propagating at infinite speed between the two subsystems.These correlations have been so far verified experimentally up to a distance of 1200 km. We discuss the interest and feasibility of extending this distance to 390,000 km, thus gaining a factor of 300. The idea is to install one of the polarimeters on the Moon, with the other on Earth. Such an experiment would provide a new test of Quantum Physics and allow to put higher constraints on alternative theories and interpretations. We also discuss the possibility to violate Bell inequalities beyond Earth-Moon distance.

AI.tldr: Proposes extending quantum entanglement tests to Earth-Moon distance (390,000 km) to gain 300x improvement over current 1200 km experiments, testing quantum physics and constraining alternative theories.
AI.motivation: Current quantum entanglement experiments have only been verified up to 1200 km. Extending this to Earth-Moon distance (390,000 km) would provide a much more stringent test of quantum physics, potentially revealing limitations or confirming predictions at unprecedented scales, while putting stronger constraints on alternative theories and interpretations that might predict distance-dependent effects.
AI.method: Proposes installing one polarimeter on the Moon and the other on Earth to perform Bell inequality tests across 390,000 km distance. The experiment would measure entangled particles generated on Earth or in space, with one subsystem measured on Earth and the other on the lunar surface.
AI.result: Theoretical proposal only - no experimental results yet. The paper discusses the feasibility and scientific interest of such an experiment, noting it would provide a 300x improvement in distance over current experiments and enable tests beyond Earth-Moon distances.
AI.conclusion: An Earth-Moon quantum entanglement experiment is scientifically valuable and potentially feasible, offering unprecedented tests of quantum physics at astronomical distances. Such an experiment could provide stronger constraints on alternative theories and interpretations while exploring the fundamental nature of quantum correlations across vast distances.

title: Assembling Extensive Quantum Fisher Information in Stabilizer Systems
comment: This work contains 5 pages and 6 figures
summary: We introduce a systematic framework to construct nonlocal observables with extensive quantum Fisher information (QFI) density in stabilizer codes. The construction maps stabilizer generators to dual Ising spins whose correlators equal string order parameters, converting hidden nonlocal order into a metrologically accessible observable. Applying this to monitored cluster codes and the toric code, we identify transitions in the QFI scaling from an extensive regime, where long-range string order prevails, to an intensive one driven by competing single-site measurements.

AI.tldr: Framework maps stabilizer codes to dual Ising spins, enabling construction of nonlocal observables with extensive quantum Fisher information density for quantum metrology applications.
AI.motivation: To develop a systematic method for constructing nonlocal observables in stabilizer codes that exhibit extensive quantum Fisher information density, enabling the conversion of hidden nonlocal order into metrologically useful quantities for quantum sensing applications.
AI.method: A framework that maps stabilizer generators to dual Ising spins, where correlators of these dual spins equal string order parameters. This mapping converts hidden nonlocal order into observable quantities with extensive QFI density.
AI.result: Application to monitored cluster codes and toric code reveals transitions in QFI scaling from extensive regime (with long-range string order) to intensive regime (driven by competing single-site measurements).
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully converts hidden nonlocal order in stabilizer codes into metrologically accessible observables, revealing measurement-driven transitions in quantum Fisher information scaling that could have implications for quantum sensing and error correction.

title: AI-Enabled Decoding of Qubit Loss for Quantum Error-Correcting Codes
summary: Qubit loss is a major source of error in quantum computation, as it invalidates the algebraic structure of the standard stabilizer formalism for quantum error-correcting codes. On the one hand, it complicates decoding; on the other hand, it introduces stochastic flicker patterns in stabilizers as a hallmark of qubit loss. Here, we develop an artificial-intelligence-enabled decoder based on a spatiotemporal Graph Neural Network (STGNN) architecture to extract spatial and temporal correlations from syndrome histories. Our decoder performs a dual-head task, simultaneously correcting standard Pauli errors and identifying the locations of qubit loss. Our decoder achieves significantly higher logical accuracy than both the traditional minimum-weight perfect matching (MWPM) algorithm and even delayed-erasure MWPM decoders that use qubit loss information from the final round as input. Our decoder can also identify more than 90% of loss locations after accumulating stabilizer measurements over the subsequent ten rounds, thereby facilitating qubit reinitialization, for instance, via the continuous loading technique on the atom array platform. For both tasks, our STGNN performs nearly identically to a modified version of AlphaQubit, but it employs a parallel input structure, giving it an advantage in inference time over modified AlphaQubit's recurrent input structure. This work provides a robust and scalable framework for correcting qubit loss errors, paving the way for more efficient fault-tolerant quantum computation.

AI.tldr: AI decoder using spatiotemporal Graph Neural Network (STGNN) simultaneously corrects Pauli errors and identifies qubit loss locations, outperforming traditional MWPM decoders and enabling efficient qubit reinitialization.
AI.motivation: Qubit loss is a major error source in quantum computation that invalidates standard stabilizer formalism, complicates decoding, and introduces stochastic flicker patterns in stabilizers, requiring specialized handling beyond traditional error correction.
AI.method: Developed an AI-enabled decoder based on spatiotemporal Graph Neural Network (STGNN) architecture that extracts spatial and temporal correlations from syndrome histories, performing dual-head task: correcting Pauli errors and identifying qubit loss locations.
AI.result: Decoder achieves significantly higher logical accuracy than traditional MWPM and delayed-erasure MWPM decoders; identifies >90% of loss locations after accumulating stabilizer measurements over ten subsequent rounds; performs nearly identically to modified AlphaQubit but with faster inference time due to parallel input structure.
AI.conclusion: Provides a robust and scalable framework for correcting qubit loss errors, enabling efficient fault-tolerant quantum computation and facilitating qubit reinitialization techniques like continuous loading on atom array platforms.

title: Entanglement and circuit complexity in finite-depth random linear optical networks
summary: We study the growth of entanglement and circuit complexity in random passive linear optical networks as a function of the circuit depth. For entanglement dynamics, we start with an initial Gaussian state with all $n$ modes squeezed. For random brickwall circuits, we show that entanglement, as measured by the Rényi-2 entropy, grows at most diffusively as a function of the depth. In the other direction, for arbitrary circuit geometries we prove bounds on depths which ensure the average subsystem entanglement reaches within a constant factor of the maximum value in all subsystems, and bounds which ensure closeness of the random linear optical unitary to a Haar random unitary in $L^2$ Wasserstein distance. We also consider robust circuit complexity for random one-dimensional brickwall circuits, as measured by the minimum number of gates required in any circuit that approximately implements the linear optical unitary. Viewing this as a function of the number of modes and the circuit depth, we show the robust circuit complexity for random one-dimensional brickwall circuits scales at most diffusively in the depth with high probability. The corresponding Gaussian unitary $\tilde{\mathcal U}$ for the approximate implementation retains high output fidelity $|\langleψ|\mathcal U^\dagger \tilde{\mathcal U}|ψ\rangle|^2$ for pure states $|ψ\rangle$ with constrained expected photon-number.

AI.tldr: Random passive linear optical networks exhibit diffusive growth of entanglement and circuit complexity with circuit depth, with bounds on depths needed for near-maximal entanglement and Haar-randomness approximation.
AI.motivation: To understand how entanglement and circuit complexity scale with depth in random passive linear optical networks, which are important for quantum information processing and quantum simulation.
AI.method: Analyze random brickwall circuits and arbitrary circuit geometries using Rényi-2 entropy for entanglement dynamics, L^2 Wasserstein distance for Haar-randomness comparison, and robust circuit complexity measures for approximate implementations.
AI.result: Entanglement grows at most diffusively with depth; bounds established for depths ensuring near-maximal entanglement in all subsystems and closeness to Haar random unitaries; robust circuit complexity scales at most diffusively in depth while maintaining high output fidelity for constrained photon-number states.
AI.conclusion: Random passive linear optical networks exhibit fundamentally diffusive scaling behavior for both entanglement growth and circuit complexity, with practical implications for quantum circuit design and certification.

title: Scalable quantum error correction tailored for a heavy-hex qubit array
summary: To produce an operable quantum computer that is made with imperfect hardware, we must design and test scalable quantum error correcting codes that are suited for the devices we can build and, in unison, develop decoding strategies that accommodate device-specific noise characteristics. Here, we introduce the \emph{dynamic compass code}, a subsystem code with a novel syndrome extraction cycle, that has a competitive threshold while making efficient use of qubits arranged on a heavy-hex lattice. We use a superconducting qubit array to implement a distance-5 instance of this code, and demonstrate how detailed noise characterisation can boost decoder performance to yield significant improvements in logical error rates. We perform averaged circuit eigenvalue sampling (ACES) to acquire detailed context-dependent error information on all elements of the syndrome extraction process. Furthermore, we leverage soft information produced from measurement devices to augment the decoder with measurement error information and detect leakage errors for exclusion through post-selection. Our noise-informed approach yields up to 38.3\% improvement in the logical error rate of a distance-5 implementation of the dynamic compass code in experiment.

AI.tldr: Dynamic compass code: a novel subsystem quantum error correcting code with efficient heavy-hex lattice implementation and noise-informed decoding that achieves 38.3% logical error rate improvement in distance-5 experimental implementation.
AI.motivation: To develop scalable quantum error correction for imperfect hardware by creating codes suited for available devices and decoding strategies that accommodate device-specific noise characteristics.
AI.method: Introduces dynamic compass code - a subsystem code with novel syndrome extraction cycle; implements distance-5 instance on superconducting qubit array; uses averaged circuit eigenvalue sampling (ACES) for detailed context-dependent error characterization; leverages soft measurement information and leakage detection for post-selection.
AI.result: Competitive threshold with efficient qubit use on heavy-hex lattice; noise-informed decoding yields up to 38.3% improvement in logical error rate for distance-5 implementation; demonstrates practical quantum error correction with detailed noise characterization.
AI.conclusion: The dynamic compass code combined with noise-informed decoding represents a significant advance toward practical quantum error correction, showing how detailed device characterization can substantially improve logical error rates in near-term quantum hardware.

title: Precision Limits of Multiparameter Markovian-Noise Metrology
comment: 39 pages (17 main text + 22 appendices). Comments and feedback are welcome
summary: Measuring stochastic signals ("noise metrology") constitutes a central task in quantum sensing and the characterization of open quantum systems. Here we establish ultimate precision bounds for multiparameter estimation of stochastic signals encoded through Markovian Lindblad dynamics, allowing for arbitrary quantum control and noiseless ancillae. Although Markovianity enforces standard-quantum-limit scaling with sensing time $T$, our bounds reveal Heisenberg-type scaling in the number of dissipative channels, $R$: when the stochastic signal exhibits high-rank correlations across the $R$ channels and the probe is entangled, the average variance (per parameter) scales no better than $Ω(1/(TR^2))$. For collective $k$-body dissipation, $R=Θ(N^k)$, signifying super-Heisenberg scaling with the system size $N$. We further show that, when the unknown parameters enter through the dissipative eigenrates, a Rapid Prepare-and-Measure (RPM) protocol that tracks many distinct quantum jumps in parallel attains these limits. In this regime, the estimation problem reduces to a multi-Poisson counting model, providing a conceptually clean route to optimal quantum noise metrology. We illustrate the breadth of the framework with applications to networked noise metrology, collective many-body dissipation, learning Pauli noise, and subdiffraction quantum imaging.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes fundamental precision bounds for multiparameter estimation of stochastic signals in Markovian quantum systems, revealing Heisenberg-type scaling in the number of dissipative channels and super-Heisenberg scaling with system size for collective dissipation.
AI.motivation: Measuring stochastic signals (noise metrology) is crucial for quantum sensing and characterizing open quantum systems, but fundamental precision limits for multiparameter estimation in Markovian dynamics with quantum control were not established.
AI.method: Developed theoretical precision bounds for multiparameter estimation of stochastic signals encoded through Markovian Lindblad dynamics, allowing arbitrary quantum control and noiseless ancillae. Proposed a Rapid Prepare-and-Measure (RPM) protocol that tracks multiple quantum jumps in parallel to achieve optimal performance.
AI.result: Revealed Heisenberg-type scaling (Ω(1/(TR²))) in the number of dissipative channels R for highly correlated signals with entangled probes. For collective k-body dissipation (R=Θ(Nᵏ)), this yields super-Heisenberg scaling with system size N. The RPM protocol achieves these fundamental limits when parameters enter through dissipative eigenrates.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides fundamental precision bounds for quantum noise metrology with optimal scaling, reduces estimation to a multi-Poisson counting model for clean analysis, and has broad applications including networked noise metrology, collective many-body dissipation, Pauli noise learning, and subdiffraction quantum imaging.

title: Low-valency scalable quantum error correction with a dynamic compass code
summary: The ongoing development of hardware that is capable of reliably executing general quantum algorithms requires quantum error-correcting codes that are both practical for realisation and rapidly reduce logical error rates as they are scaled up. Here we introduce the dynamic compass code, a code that can be implemented with a modest footprint on the heavy-hex lattice while also demonstrating a threshold. The dynamic code is obtained by choosing a novel measurement schedule for the syndrome extraction circuit of the heavy-hex subsystem code. We numerically evaluate its performance and observe that different choices of schedule can provide a trade-off in protection against logical errors in the $X$ vs $Z$ basis. We also demonstrate that this new measurement schedule provides the code with a threshold for stability experiments. We finally show how the dynamic compass code could be used for fault-tolerant logic by illustrating lattice surgery between code patches.

AI.tldr: Dynamic compass code: a novel quantum error-correcting code implementable on heavy-hex lattice with threshold, offering trade-off in X/Z basis protection and enabling fault-tolerant logic via lattice surgery.
AI.motivation: Need for practical quantum error-correcting codes that are realizable on existing hardware (heavy-hex lattice) while providing rapid error rate reduction as they scale, requiring both implementation feasibility and threshold behavior.
AI.method: Introduces dynamic compass code by designing novel measurement schedule for syndrome extraction circuit of heavy-hex subsystem code, enabling implementation on heavy-hex lattice with modest footprint.
AI.result: Numerical evaluation shows: 1) Different measurement schedules provide trade-off in protection against X vs Z basis logical errors, 2) Code exhibits threshold in stability experiments, 3) Enables fault-tolerant logic via lattice surgery between code patches.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic compass code represents a practical quantum error-correcting solution for near-term hardware, combining implementability on heavy-hex lattice with threshold behavior and flexible error protection trade-offs, enabling fault-tolerant quantum computation.

title: Cell-Dependent Criticality for Quantum Metrology
summary: Exploiting enhanced sensitivity of a system in the vicinity of a phase transition boundary, critical quantum metrology to date still suffers from gap-closure related bottleneck effects, namely, critical slowing down of the sensing dynamics and a drastic shrinking of the parameter sensing window. To alleviate the said bottleneck inherent to any homogeneous lattice used for sensing, here we propose to leverage the intrinsic hopping inhomogeneity arising from bosonic ladder-operator matrix elements in Fock-space lattices (FSLs). Specifically, using a two-mode Jaynes--Cummings-type model, we show that the sensing parameter can be imprinted onto a topological zero-energy mode of the FSL. The key system parameters thus become cell dependent, effectively tracing out a curve in a topological phase diagram. Cell-dependent criticality emerges when this curve crosses or approaches a topological phase boundary, without globally tuning the lattice close to criticality. An external control parameter reshapes this curve, continuously tuning the scaling of the quantum Fisher information from the standard to the Heisenberg scaling while maintaining broad sensing coverage and a reduced gap cost. Furthermore, a local photon-number measurement on a single cavity saturates the quantum Fisher information. These results identify FSLs as a scalable and practical route to criticality-based quantum metrology.

AI.tldr: FSL-based critical quantum metrology overcomes gap-closure bottlenecks by leveraging hopping inhomogeneity in Fock-space lattices, enabling cell-dependent criticality without global critical tuning and achieving Heisenberg scaling with broad sensing coverage.
AI.motivation: Critical quantum metrology suffers from gap-closure bottleneck effects: critical slowing down of sensing dynamics and drastic shrinking of parameter sensing windows when using homogeneous lattices near phase transitions.
AI.method: Propose leveraging intrinsic hopping inhomogeneity from bosonic ladder-operator matrix elements in Fock-space lattices. Use two-mode Jaynes-Cummings-type model to imprint sensing parameter onto topological zero-energy mode of FSL, creating cell-dependent criticality where system parameters trace a curve crossing topological phase boundaries.
AI.result: External control parameter reshapes curve to continuously tune quantum Fisher information scaling from standard to Heisenberg scaling while maintaining broad sensing coverage and reduced gap cost. Local photon-number measurement on single cavity saturates quantum Fisher information.
AI.conclusion: FSLs provide scalable and practical route to criticality-based quantum metrology by overcoming traditional bottleneck effects through cell-dependent criticality without requiring global critical tuning.

title: Warring Contextualities - Provably Classical vs Provably Nonclassical
summary: In the literature, there are two differing definitions of contextuality: Kochen and Specker's, and Spekkens' (or ``generalised''). However, researchers using one of these definitions rarely consider the other, meaning comparative analysis of these two notions is rare. In this paper, we advance the idea that Kochen-Specker contextuality provides a generalisation of the idea of system being fundamentally nonclassical, while Spekkens' noncontextuality provides a generalisation of the idea of a system being classical. This allows us to reconcile the two approaches, as different stages in a hierarchy of classicality/nonclassicality.

AI.tldr: The paper reconciles two definitions of contextuality (Kochen-Specker and Spekkens') by positioning them as different stages in a hierarchy of classicality/nonclassicality.
AI.motivation: The two definitions of contextuality (Kochen-Specker and Spekkens') are rarely considered together in the literature, creating a gap in comparative analysis between these important notions.
AI.method: The authors propose a conceptual framework where Kochen-Specker contextuality generalizes the idea of a system being fundamentally nonclassical, while Spekkens' noncontextuality generalizes the idea of a system being classical.
AI.result: The paper establishes that the two approaches can be reconciled as different stages in a hierarchy of classicality/nonclassicality, providing a unified perspective on contextuality.
AI.conclusion: The two definitions of contextuality are complementary rather than contradictory, representing different levels in a classicality hierarchy, which resolves the apparent conflict between them.

title: Boson sampling beyond the dilute regime: second moments and anti-concentration
summary: Boson sampling is a leading candidate for demonstrating quantum advantage in photonic systems. Despite significant experimental and theoretical progress, a characterization of its output statistics remains incomplete. This is especially true beyond the dilute regime, where photon collisions and bunching become significant. The associated saturated regime, characterized by mode number growing linearly with photon number, or more generally sub-quadratically, is precisely the regime of greatest experimental interest. As a consequence, anti-concentration of the output distribution--a key ingredient in hardness arguments--remains poorly understood in boson sampling. In this work, we leverage representation-theoretic tools to address this gap, obtaining closed-form expressions for second moments of generic particle-number-preserving bosonic observables. We express these quantities in terms of Hilbert-Schmidt norms of projections onto irreducible components of the operator space and show that these projection norms admit compact analytical expressions by exploiting the underlying symmetry structure. Focusing on Fock state output probabilities, we further establish anti-concentration beyond the dilute regime. Together with recent complexity-theoretic results, our findings strengthen hardness guarantees for boson sampling in experimentally interesting settings.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses anti-concentration in boson sampling beyond the dilute regime using representation-theoretic methods to obtain closed-form expressions for second moments of bosonic observables, strengthening hardness guarantees for experimental settings.
AI.motivation: Boson sampling is a leading candidate for quantum advantage, but characterization of output statistics remains incomplete, especially beyond the dilute regime where photon collisions and bunching become significant. The saturated regime (mode number growing linearly with photon number) is of greatest experimental interest, yet anti-concentration—a key ingredient in hardness arguments—remains poorly understood in this regime.
AI.method: The authors leverage representation-theoretic tools to obtain closed-form expressions for second moments of generic particle-number-preserving bosonic observables. They express these quantities in terms of Hilbert-Schmidt norms of projections onto irreducible components of the operator space and show these projection norms admit compact analytical expressions by exploiting the underlying symmetry structure.
AI.result: The paper establishes anti-concentration beyond the dilute regime for Fock state output probabilities. By obtaining analytical expressions for second moments and projection norms, the work provides mathematical tools to characterize output statistics in the experimentally relevant saturated regime.
AI.conclusion: Together with recent complexity-theoretic results, these findings strengthen hardness guarantees for boson sampling in experimentally interesting settings, addressing a key gap in understanding anti-concentration beyond the dilute regime and providing representation-theoretic tools for analyzing bosonic systems.

title: Learning Cut Distributions with Quantum Optimization
summary: Many combinatorial optimization problems admit a maximin fairness variant, where the aim is to find a distribution over possible solutions which maximizes an expected worst-case outcome. However, the support for an optimal distribution may be exponential, which can be intractable to represent in the worst case. To this end, we propose a quantum based approach to solving distribution optimization problems. Expanding on work analyzing the Dynamical Lie Algebras of the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA), we show that with a finite number of layers, a QAOA ansatz can be constructed to capture any distribution over bitstrings. We show that the resulting circuit is able to effectively solve the Fair Cut Cover, a fair interpretation of the classical Fractional Cut Cover Problem. In addition, we show that our algorithm is provably better than classical approximations on certain graph structures and empirically outperforms these classical algorithms on tested instances.

AI.tldr: Quantum algorithm using QAOA to solve combinatorial optimization problems with maximin fairness constraints by encoding distributions over solutions in quantum circuits.
AI.motivation: Combinatorial optimization problems with maximin fairness constraints often require exponential support for optimal distributions, making classical representation intractable. A quantum approach is needed to efficiently handle these distribution optimization problems.
AI.method: Extends QAOA dynamical Lie algebra analysis to construct ansatz with finite layers that can capture any distribution over bitstrings. Applies this to solve Fair Cut Cover (fair variant of Fractional Cut Cover Problem).
AI.result: The quantum circuit effectively solves Fair Cut Cover, provably outperforms classical approximations on certain graph structures, and empirically beats classical algorithms on tested instances.
AI.conclusion: Quantum approaches using QAOA can efficiently solve distribution optimization problems with fairness constraints, offering advantages over classical methods for certain combinatorial optimization problems.

title: Distributed Learning of Quantum State Tomography Robust to Readout Errors
summary: Scalable estimation of quantum states with readout errors is a central challenge in large multiqubit systems. Existing overlapping-tomography methods improve scalability by working with local subsystems, but they usually assume known or separately calibrated measurements. At the same time, readout-estimation methods model measurement errors without enforcing consistency among overlapping regional states. In this context, the present paper introduces a unified framework for joint regional quantum state tomography with readout errors. A multiqubit system is partitioned in overlapping regions, each region is assigned to a local density operator and a local confusion matrix, and neighboring regions are coupled through reduced-state consistency on shared subsystems. This leads to a structured bilinear optimization problem. To solve it, a distributed alternating method is developed in which the state-update step is handled by the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM), while the confusion-matrix updates are carried out locally in parallel. Analytical guarantees are also established, including a sufficient condition for local identifiability, local quadratic growth of the population misfit, and convergence of the inner state-update procedure. Simulations on Ring, Ladder, Torus, and Hub graph geometries show that joint estimation improves state recovery over fixed-readout reconstruction, recovers a substantial portion of oracle performance, and reveals a clear tradeoff between state estimation performance, communication, and computation.

AI.tldr: Unified framework for joint regional quantum state tomography with readout errors using overlapping regions, local density operators and confusion matrices, solved via distributed alternating optimization with ADMM.
AI.motivation: Existing methods either assume known measurements (overlapping tomography) or model readout errors without enforcing consistency among overlapping regional states, creating a need for unified joint estimation.
AI.method: Partition multiqubit system into overlapping regions with local density operators and confusion matrices, enforce consistency through reduced-state constraints, solve structured bilinear optimization via distributed alternating method using ADMM for state updates and parallel local confusion-matrix updates.
AI.result: Joint estimation improves state recovery over fixed-readout reconstruction, recovers substantial portion of oracle performance, reveals tradeoff between state estimation performance, communication, and computation across Ring, Ladder, Torus, and Hub geometries.
AI.conclusion: Proposed unified framework successfully addresses joint regional quantum state tomography with readout errors, providing analytical guarantees and demonstrating practical advantages through distributed optimization and consistency enforcement.

title: Distributed Variational Quantum Linear Solver
summary: The Variational Quantum Linear Solver (VQLS), a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm for solving linear systems, faces a practical scalability bottleneck: the Linear Combination of Unitaries (LCU) decomposition requires O(L^2) circuit evaluations per optimizer iteration, where $L$ can grow as 4^n for n-qubit systems for the worst case scenario. We address this computational bottleneck through two complementary strategies. First, we present a distributed VQLS (D-VQLS) framework, built on NVIDIA CUDA-Q, that enables asynchronous, scalable distribution of the O(L^2) cost-function evaluations. Second, a fast Walsh--Hadamard transform (FWHT)-based Pauli decomposition with 1% coefficient thresholding curbs the exponential growth of LCU terms, reducing L from O}(2^n) to O(1) for n > 6 qubits and compressing the per-iteration circuit complexity from O(n * 4^n) to O(n) for sparse, structured matrices. For a 10-qubit tridiagonal Toeplitz system, this yields a 256x reduction, from 23 million to 90,112 circuits per iteration, while preserving over $99.99\%$ solution fidelity. Additionally, to inform feasibility on early fault-tolerant QPUs, the paper provides resource estimates -- gate counts, qubit requirements, and circuit evaluations per iteration -- for VQLS applied to arbitrary matrices. The D-VQLS framework is validated on the NERSC Perlmutter supercomputer using multi-node, multi-GPU ideal state-vector simulations, achieving over 99.99% fidelity against classical solutions on tridiagonal Toeplitz and Hele--Shaw flow benchmarks, with near-ideal strong scaling up to 24 GPUs and 95.3% weak scaling efficiency at 96 GPUs processing 360,448 circuits per iteration for a 10-qubit system. Systematic profiling identifies the optimal resource allocation for distributed quantum circuit workloads, yielding a 2.52x speedup for the configurations studied.

AI.tldr: VQLS algorithm faces O(L²) circuit evaluation bottleneck; distributed framework (D-VQLS) and FWHT-based Pauli decomposition with thresholding reduce complexity from O(n·4ⁿ) to O(n), achieving 256x reduction for 10-qubit systems while maintaining >99.99% fidelity.
AI.motivation: The Variational Quantum Linear Solver (VQLS) suffers from a practical scalability bottleneck where the Linear Combination of Unitaries (LCU) decomposition requires O(L²) circuit evaluations per optimizer iteration, with L potentially growing as 4ⁿ for n-qubit systems in worst-case scenarios, making the algorithm computationally prohibitive for larger systems.
AI.method: Two complementary strategies: (1) Distributed VQLS (D-VQLS) framework built on NVIDIA CUDA-Q enabling asynchronous, scalable distribution of O(L²) cost-function evaluations; (2) Fast Walsh-Hadamard transform (FWHT)-based Pauli decomposition with 1% coefficient thresholding to curb exponential growth of LCU terms, reducing L from O(2ⁿ) to O(1) for n > 6 qubits and compressing per-iteration circuit complexity from O(n·4ⁿ) to O(n) for sparse, structured matrices.
AI.result: For a 10-qubit tridiagonal Toeplitz system: 256x reduction from 23 million to 90,112 circuits per iteration while preserving >99.99% solution fidelity. D-VQLS validated on NERSC Perlmutter supercomputer with multi-node, multi-GPU ideal state-vector simulations achieved >99.99% fidelity against classical solutions on benchmarks, with near-ideal strong scaling up to 24 GPUs and 95.3% weak scaling efficiency at 96 GPUs processing 360,448 circuits per iteration. Systematic profiling yielded 2.52x speedup for studied configurations.
AI.conclusion: The combined D-VQLS framework and FWHT-based Pauli decomposition with thresholding effectively addresses the scalability bottleneck of VQLS, enabling practical implementation on early fault-tolerant quantum processing units while maintaining high solution fidelity and demonstrating efficient distributed execution on classical HPC systems.

title: Performance Optimization Method for Laser-Phase-Noise based Quantum Random Number Generation
summary: The quantum random number generation based on laser phase noise, which is featured with high generation rate and ease for photonic integration, has been extensively investigated and demonstrated. Despite these advancements, a theoretical model to achieve optimal performance in terms of maximizing the generation rate or entropy is still incomplete. In this work, a comprehensive physical model for this scheme is introduced to accurately predict the power spectrum and probability distribution of raw data, based on which the entropy source bandwidth and quantum min-entropy can be accordingly calculated and thus the system performance can be quantitatively evaluated. The model is sufficiently validated through both simulation and experiment with significant agreement under various setups. Furthermore, our proposal enables the priori configuration of experimental setups to achieve designed power spectrum and probability distribution of the raw data, thereby maximizing the generation rate or the min-entropy for system performance optimization.

AI.tldr: Theoretical model for optimizing quantum random number generation from laser phase noise, enabling performance prediction and experimental configuration for maximum generation rate or entropy.
AI.motivation: Quantum random number generation based on laser phase noise offers high generation rates and photonic integration potential, but lacks a complete theoretical model for achieving optimal performance in terms of maximizing generation rate or entropy.
AI.method: Developed a comprehensive physical model that accurately predicts the power spectrum and probability distribution of raw data from laser phase noise, enabling calculation of entropy source bandwidth and quantum min-entropy for system performance evaluation.
AI.result: The model was validated through both simulation and experiment with significant agreement under various setups, demonstrating its accuracy in predicting system behavior.
AI.conclusion: The proposed model enables a priori configuration of experimental setups to achieve designed power spectrum and probability distribution, thereby maximizing generation rate or min-entropy for system performance optimization in quantum random number generation.

title: Dual-mode ground-state cooling in quadratic optomechanical systems: from multistability to general dark-mode suppression
summary: We theoretically investigate a quadratic optomechanical system comprising a single-mode optical cavity linearly coupled to one mechanical resonator and quadratically coupled to a second resonator. By tuning the cavity detuning and optomechanical coupling strengths, we demonstrate the transition from optical bistability to multistability with up to seven steady-state solutions. Notably, simultaneous ground-state cooling of both mechanical resonators occurs on the dynamically stable branch of the nonlinear steady-state solutions, offering new opportunities for combined nonlinear optical and quantum cooling functionalities. Beyond the multistable regime, we systematically study dual-mode ground-state cooling and find that robust simultaneous cooling can be achieved over a broad parameter range, except when the linear and quadratic couplings become comparable, where a dark-mode effect arises. In this case, tuning the second-order optomechanical-induced frequency shifts effectively suppresses dark-mode interference, enabling controllable and simultaneous ground-state cooling. Our results provide a versatile framework for engineering multimode quantum states in optomechanical systems and open new avenues for the development of multifunctional quantum devices, including ultra-sensitive sensors, scalable quantum memories, and integrated quantum networks.

AI.tldr: Quadratic optomechanical system with linear and quadratic couplings exhibits transition from optical bistability to multistability (up to 7 steady states) and enables simultaneous ground-state cooling of both mechanical resonators.
AI.motivation: To explore nonlinear dynamics and quantum control in multimode optomechanical systems with both linear and quadratic couplings, aiming to develop multifunctional quantum devices like sensors, memories, and quantum networks.
AI.method: Theoretical investigation of a single-mode optical cavity linearly coupled to one mechanical resonator and quadratically coupled to a second resonator, with tuning of cavity detuning and optomechanical coupling strengths to study transitions between different dynamical regimes.
AI.result: Demonstrated transition from optical bistability to multistability with up to seven steady-state solutions; achieved simultaneous ground-state cooling of both mechanical resonators on dynamically stable branches; identified dark-mode effect when linear and quadratic couplings become comparable; showed that tuning second-order optomechanical-induced frequency shifts suppresses dark-mode interference for controllable simultaneous cooling.
AI.conclusion: The system provides a versatile framework for engineering multimode quantum states and opens new avenues for multifunctional quantum devices including ultra-sensitive sensors, scalable quantum memories, and integrated quantum networks.

title: Linear Optical Schemes to Postselect High-Dimensional Dicke States
summary: Multipartite entanglement is an essential quantum resource for various distributed quantum applications. One promising method for preparing multipartite entanglement is to interfere independent photons at linear optical interference setups. While heralding the successful interference and thereby the state generation is often costly, postselecting entangled states provides an achievable alternative in this framework. We introduce a family of interference schemes for postselecting symmetric qudit Dicke states, useful resources in quantum communication and variational quantum computing. We present schemes with and without ancillary photons and show that using ancillary photons can exceed the upper bound on the success probability of schemes without ancillary photons. Our results accommodate a wide range of linear optical schemes, providing multiple viable approaches for postselecting Dicke states.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces linear optical interference schemes for postselecting symmetric qudit Dicke states, showing that using ancillary photons can exceed success probability bounds of schemes without ancillas.
AI.motivation: Multipartite entanglement is essential for distributed quantum applications, and linear optical interference provides a promising method for preparing such states. While heralding successful interference is often costly, postselection offers a more achievable alternative for generating entangled states.
AI.method: The authors introduce a family of interference schemes using linear optical setups to postselect symmetric qudit Dicke states. They present schemes both with and without ancillary photons, analyzing their performance and success probabilities.
AI.result: The results show that using ancillary photons can exceed the upper bound on success probability achievable by schemes without ancillary photons. The proposed schemes accommodate various linear optical configurations, providing multiple viable approaches for postselecting Dicke states.
AI.conclusion: The introduced family of interference schemes offers practical methods for generating symmetric qudit Dicke states via postselection in linear optical systems, with ancillary-enhanced schemes providing higher success probabilities than ancilla-free approaches.

title: Observation of Tunable Superradiant Frequency Combs
summary: Cavity quantum electrodynamics (QED) with quantum emitters coupled to resonators provides a powerful platform for engineering light-matter interactions and exploring collective phenomena. In particular, superradiance, arising from collective quantum interference among emitters, has been explored as a route to ultrastable continuous radiation. However, engineering superradiance in the time domain to realize periodic pulsed sources or frequency combs remains largely unexplored. Here, we investigate the non-equilibrium many-body dynamics of a driven spin ensemble coupled to an on-chip superconducting resonator and uncover a dynamical phase transition from continuous-wave to periodic pulsed superradiant emission. To quantitatively capture the observed dynamical phases, we introduce a driven-dissipative cavity-QED model that elucidates how the periodic pulsed superradiant phase emerges from collective, periodically repeating spin dynamics stabilized by the interplay of coherence growth, disorder, and dissipation. We also find that rare-earth ion spin systems exhibiting both optical and microwave transitions enable phase-synchronized, dual-rail superradiant frequency combs in both the microwave and optical domains. Our results not only open new avenues for dual-rail frequency-comb applications in quantum metrology and information processing, but also establish a fundamental connection between periodic pulsed superradiance and the emergence of a continuous time crystal as a novel nonequilibrium phase in driven open systems.

AI.tldr: Researchers discovered a dynamical phase transition from continuous-wave to periodic pulsed superradiant emission in a driven spin ensemble coupled to a superconducting resonator, enabling dual-rail frequency combs and connecting pulsed superradiance to continuous time crystals.
AI.motivation: While cavity QED enables engineering of light-matter interactions and superradiance for ultrastable continuous radiation, engineering superradiance in the time domain to create periodic pulsed sources or frequency combs remains largely unexplored.
AI.method: Investigated non-equilibrium many-body dynamics of a driven spin ensemble coupled to an on-chip superconducting resonator, using a driven-dissipative cavity-QED model to capture the observed dynamical phases, with rare-earth ion spin systems exhibiting both optical and microwave transitions.
AI.result: Discovered a dynamical phase transition from continuous-wave to periodic pulsed superradiant emission, where periodic pulsed superradiant phase emerges from collective, periodically repeating spin dynamics stabilized by coherence growth, disorder, and dissipation interplay, enabling phase-synchronized dual-rail superradiant frequency combs in both microwave and optical domains.
AI.conclusion: The results open new avenues for dual-rail frequency-comb applications in quantum metrology and information processing, and establish a fundamental connection between periodic pulsed superradiance and the emergence of continuous time crystals as a novel nonequilibrium phase in driven open systems.

title: Optimal Quantum Logarithmic Trace Inequality
summary: We establish a sharp logarithmic trace inequality that strengthens recent bounds of Cheng et al.(arXiv:2507.07961) by replacing their prefactor $c_s/s$ with the strictly smaller constant $G_s$. The constant $G_s$ is defined via the scalar inequality $\log(1+r)\le G_s r^s$ and admits a closed-form expression in terms of the Lambert $W$ function. Our approach introduces an iterative integration-by-parts procedure that lifts optimal scalar bounds to the operator level without loss. We prove that $G_s$ is the optimal universal constant, in the sense that no smaller constant satisfies the inequality for all positive operators. For density matrices, this optimality persists up to $s\le \frac{1}{2\log(2)}$, while beyond this threshold the commuting case exhibits a strictly smaller optimal constant and the noncommuting case remains open. In the regime $s\to0$, our result improves the prefactor $c_s/s$ of Cheng et al. by a factor of $1/e$. These sharper inequalities enhance key primitives in quantum information theory, including decoupling, convex-splitting, and covering lemmas, leading to tighter finite-resource bounds.

AI.tldr: Sharp logarithmic trace inequality with optimal constant G_s improves previous bounds, enhances quantum information primitives.
AI.motivation: To strengthen recent logarithmic trace inequality bounds by Cheng et al. by replacing their prefactor c_s/s with a strictly smaller optimal constant G_s, leading to tighter finite-resource bounds in quantum information theory.
AI.method: Introduces an iterative integration-by-parts procedure that lifts optimal scalar bounds to the operator level without loss, using the Lambert W function to define G_s via the scalar inequality log(1+r) ≤ G_s r^s.
AI.result: Proves G_s is the optimal universal constant for all positive operators; for density matrices, optimality persists up to s ≤ 1/(2log(2)); beyond this threshold, commuting case has strictly smaller optimal constant while noncommuting case remains open; improves prefactor by factor 1/e as s→0.
AI.conclusion: The sharper inequalities enhance key primitives in quantum information theory including decoupling, convex-splitting, and covering lemmas, leading to tighter finite-resource bounds.

title: Photonic state engineering via energy-level crossing by giant atoms in topological waveguide QED setup
summary: Photonic state engineering in waveguide QED is typically based on local light-matter interactions. This limits its control over the spatial structure of bound photonic states. Here, we demonstrate a distinct mechanism arising from the interplay between nonlocal giant-atom coupling and topological band structure. Specifically, we consider giant atoms coupled to a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger waveguide and show that this configuration enables a controllable energy-level crossing protected by the topological gap. Adiabatically sweeping the atomic detuning across the crossing leads to a controlled exchange between distinct photonic bound states. In a two-giant-atom configuration, this mechanism achieves high-fidelity conversion of a spatially splitting state into a combining state. Extending this scheme to three-giant atoms, we further realize robust, shape-preserving photon transfer mediated by sequential in-gap crossings. Our results demonstrate how topology and nonlocal light-matter coupling can be combined to achieve programmable control of bound photonic states in waveguide QED platforms.

AI.tldr: Nonlocal giant-atom coupling combined with topological SSH waveguide enables programmable control of bound photonic states through protected energy-level crossings and adiabatic state transfer.
AI.motivation: Traditional waveguide QED uses local light-matter interactions, limiting spatial control of bound photonic states. The paper aims to overcome this by combining nonlocal giant-atom coupling with topological band structures for enhanced control capabilities.
AI.method: Giant atoms coupled to a Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) topological waveguide, enabling controllable energy-level crossings protected by topological gaps. Adiabatic sweeping of atomic detuning across these crossings facilitates controlled exchange between distinct photonic bound states.
AI.result: Two-giant-atom configuration achieves high-fidelity conversion between spatially splitting and combining states. Three-giant-atom extension realizes robust, shape-preserving photon transfer through sequential in-gap crossings.
AI.conclusion: Topology and nonlocal light-matter coupling can be synergistically combined to achieve programmable control of bound photonic states in waveguide QED platforms, overcoming limitations of local interactions.

title: Spectroscopic measurement of the Casimir-Polder force in the intermediate regime
summary: The Casimir-Polder (CP) effect -- the force between a neutral atom and an uncharged conducting plate in empty space -- is an intriguing consequence of quantum vacuum fluctuations. The typically attractive CP potential crosses over from a scaling of $z^{-3}$ at short separations to $z^{-4}$ at long distances, where retardation effects due to the finite speed of light become important. At intermediate distances, where the atom--surface separation is of the order of the wavelength of the dominant atomic transition, experiments have so far relied on indirect methods, such as diffraction or quantum reflection, to observe the CP effect. Here, we directly reveal the CP force between strontium atoms and a dielectric surface via the induced shifts in the atomic energy levels in the intermediate regime. We spectroscopically probe the CP-induced kHz-frequency shift of ultracold atoms confined by a magic-wavelength optical lattice at 189(2)~nm from the surface -- on the scale of the dominant 461-nm transition. Our measurements agree well with QED calculations and differ from the short-range approximation, while excluding the long-distance one. This paves the way for studying the CP effect across various surface properties and geometries, as well as exploring the tensor nature of the atom-surface potential -- all important for the development of hybrid atomic optical-magnetic quantum devices.

AI.tldr: Direct spectroscopic measurement of Casimir-Polder force between strontium atoms and dielectric surface at intermediate distances (~189 nm), revealing kHz-level energy shifts that match QED calculations.
AI.motivation: Previous experiments on Casimir-Polder effect at intermediate distances (where atom-surface separation is comparable to dominant atomic transition wavelength) relied on indirect methods like diffraction or quantum reflection. There was a need for direct measurements in this regime to validate theoretical predictions.
AI.method: Used ultracold strontium atoms confined by a magic-wavelength optical lattice at 189(2) nm from a dielectric surface. Spectroscopically probed the CP-induced kHz-frequency shift of atomic energy levels, with separation on the scale of the dominant 461-nm transition.
AI.result: Directly revealed CP force via induced energy level shifts in intermediate regime. Measurements agree well with QED calculations, differ from short-range approximation, and exclude long-distance approximation. Observed kHz-frequency shifts at 189 nm separation.
AI.conclusion: Successfully demonstrated direct measurement of Casimir-Polder effect at intermediate distances, paving way for studying CP effect across various surface properties/geometries and exploring tensor nature of atom-surface potential for hybrid atomic optical-magnetic quantum devices.

title: Quantum Mpemba Effect in Non-Equilibrium Quantum Thermometry
summary: The quantum Mpemba effect (QMpE) describes an anomalous thermalization phenomenon in which quantum states initially far from equilibrium can approach thermal equilibrium faster than states that begin closer to it. While this effect has been extensively studied in various frameworks, its practical implications for quantum information processing remain largely unexplored. We investigate the relationship between QMpE and quantum thermometry, focusing on non-equilibrium scenarios where measurements are performed during early-stage thermalization. In a Markovian model, we rigorously prove that the initial states that are optimal for thermometry exhibit QMpE with high probability and thermalize faster than most initial states. Our results reveal a fundamental connection between quantum thermodynamics and thermometry, suggesting that QMpE can be harnessed to enhance temperature estimation with quantum probes.

AI.tldr: Quantum Mpemba effect (QMpE) enhances quantum thermometry by enabling faster thermalization of optimal temperature estimation states.
AI.motivation: While QMpE has been studied theoretically, its practical applications in quantum information processing, particularly quantum thermometry, remain unexplored. The paper aims to investigate the connection between QMpE and temperature estimation in non-equilibrium scenarios.
AI.method: The study uses a Markovian model to analyze early-stage thermalization. The authors rigorously prove that initial states optimal for thermometry exhibit QMpE with high probability and thermalize faster than most initial states.
AI.result: The research demonstrates a fundamental connection between quantum thermodynamics and thermometry, showing that QMpE can be harnessed to enhance temperature estimation with quantum probes.
AI.conclusion: QMpE provides a practical advantage for quantum thermometry by enabling faster thermalization of optimal temperature estimation states, bridging quantum thermodynamics with quantum information processing applications.

title: Activating entanglement and EPR steering from continuous-variable resources using witness-based measures
summary: We introduce a general witness-based framework for quantifying and operationally activating continuous-variable (CV) resources into discrete-variable (DV) bipartite entanglement or Einstein- Podolsky-Rosen (EPR) steering. For the three standard CV resource theories associated with Wigner negativity (WN), genuine non-Gaussianity (GNG), and standard non-Gaussianity (SNG), we define infinite families of bounded-witness monotones indexed by box constraints on the witness operators. For closed convex free sets, these monotones are faithful, strongly monotonic under free instruments, Lipschitz continuous, and convex. For closed nonconvex free sets, we show that faithfulness requires a two-copy lift and formulate the corresponding strong-monotonicity statement in the lifted theory. We further construct witness-dependent completely positive trace-preserving (CPTP) measure-and-prepare channels whose outputs are two-qubit Werner states. For the representative case n = m = 1, the optimal entanglement and EPR steering attainable within this witness-dependent activation family are exactly proportional to the underlying monotones. We illustrate the framework with odd-parity states, pure-loss single-photon states, and Gottesman- Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) states, and derive explicit lower bounds for pure-state GNG and SNG. More broadly, our results show that closed CV free sets admit witness-based quantifiers with a direct operational interpretation in terms of experimentally accessible DV correlations.

AI.tldr: A witness-based framework for quantifying and activating continuous-variable resources into discrete-variable entanglement or EPR steering, with applications to Wigner negativity, genuine non-Gaussianity, and standard non-Gaussianity resource theories.
AI.motivation: To develop a general framework for quantifying continuous-variable (CV) resources and operationally activating them into discrete-variable (DV) bipartite entanglement or EPR steering, providing experimentally accessible measures with direct operational interpretations.
AI.method: Introduces infinite families of bounded-witness monotones indexed by box constraints on witness operators; defines witness-dependent CPTP measure-and-prepare channels that output two-qubit Werner states; analyzes both convex and nonconvex free sets with two-copy lifts for faithfulness in nonconvex cases.
AI.result: For closed convex free sets, the monotones are faithful, strongly monotonic under free instruments, Lipschitz continuous, and convex. For n=m=1 case, optimal entanglement and EPR steering attainable are exactly proportional to the underlying monotones. Explicit lower bounds derived for pure-state genuine non-Gaussianity and standard non-Gaussianity.
AI.conclusion: The framework shows that closed CV free sets admit witness-based quantifiers with direct operational interpretation in terms of experimentally accessible DV correlations, enabling resource quantification and activation across CV resource theories.

title: Integrable, Mixed, and Chaotic Dynamics in a Single All-to-All Ising Spin Model
comment: 18 pages, 7 figures. Accepted in Journal of Physics A: Mathematical and Theoretical
summary: We demonstrate that the Ising all-to-all (ATA) model exhibits a range of dynamics, from integrable to chaotic, including mixed behaviour across symmetry blocks within a single system. While other works have explored the dynamics of all-to-all systems by varying parameters, we analyse a fixed set of parameters and examine the dynamics within different blocks. In addition to investigating the dynamical properties, we show that the system remains resilient to noise when the norm of the Hamiltonian representing the noise is close to 1. Our results are presented by mapping each symmetry sector of the system to a kicked top (KT) and observing that KT parameters for each sector depend on its dimension. This system, similar to the Bunimovich billiard for classical chaos, provides a new platform for studying dynamics determined by the symmetry sector, advancing quantum chaos research.

AI.tldr: The Ising all-to-all model shows integrable to chaotic dynamics across different symmetry blocks within a fixed parameter set, with resilience to noise when noise Hamiltonian norm is near 1, mapped to kicked top systems.
AI.motivation: To explore how quantum systems can exhibit different dynamical behaviors (integrable to chaotic) within different symmetry blocks of the same system under fixed parameters, advancing quantum chaos research beyond parameter variation approaches.
