Beyond query complexity, we also analyze how these improvements translate into gate costs and show that the query-optimal strategy is not always optimal in practice from the perspective of gate complexity. Our results provide both a query-optimal algorithm and a practically optimal choice of strategy depending on hardware capabilities, along with a complete landscape of intermediate methods to guide practitioners. All algorithms require only amplitude estimation as a subroutine and are thus natural candidates for early-fault-tolerant implementations.

AI.tldr: Quantum kernel method inference optimized via amplitude estimation and single-observable encoding achieves query complexity O(∥α∥₁/ε), removing N dependence and providing quadratic improvements.
AI.motivation: Standard quantum kernel inference suffers from high query complexity O(N∥α∥₂²/ε²) due to independent sampling of N kernel terms, creating a bottleneck for practical quantum advantage in supervised learning.
AI.method: Systematically analyze combinations of kernel estimation methods (sampling vs amplitude estimation) and sum approximation strategies (term-by-term vs single observable). The optimal approach encodes the full inference sum as a single observable expectation value and applies quantum amplitude estimation.
AI.result: Achieves query complexity O(∥α∥₁/ε), removing N dependence and providing quadratic improvements in both ∥α∥₁ and ε. Proves matching lower bound Ω(∥α∥₁/ε), establishing query-optimality up to logarithmic factors. Also analyzes gate complexity trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Provides both query-optimal algorithm and practical strategy selection based on hardware capabilities, with complete landscape of intermediate methods. All algorithms use only amplitude estimation, making them suitable for early-fault-tolerant quantum implementations.

title: Universal quantum state purification with energy-preserving operations
summary: Quantum state purification, which operates not by identifying and correcting specific errors but by repeatedly projecting multiple noisy copies onto special subspaces, provides a syndrome-free alternative to quantum error correction. Existing purification protocols, however, generally assume unconstrained operations and thus overlook the energetic restrictions inherent in realistic quantum devices. Here, we establish a general framework for universal state purification under energy-conservation constraints for depolarizing noise. We derive a necessary and sufficient condition for the nonexistence of universal energy-preserving purification and, whenever such purification is feasible, analytically determine the optimal performance and the corresponding protocols. We further show how the optimal protocols can be systematically implemented using only energy-preserving operations. Numerical results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed scheme. Our framework recovers the standard purification setting as a special case and naturally extends to scenarios assisted by external energy resources. These results identify fundamental physical limits on state distillation and provide an energy-efficient route to quantum error mitigation.

AI.tldr: Establishes framework for quantum state purification under energy-conservation constraints, deriving conditions for feasibility and optimal protocols for depolarizing noise.
AI.motivation: Existing purification protocols overlook energetic restrictions inherent in realistic quantum devices, creating need for energy-efficient purification methods.
AI.method: Develops general framework for universal state purification under energy-conservation constraints, derives necessary/sufficient conditions for feasibility, analytically determines optimal performance and protocols, and shows systematic implementation using only energy-preserving operations.
AI.result: Numerical results confirm effectiveness; framework recovers standard purification as special case and extends to scenarios with external energy resources.
AI.conclusion: Identifies fundamental physical limits on state distillation and provides energy-efficient route to quantum error mitigation.

title: IQP circuits for 2-Forrelation
summary: The 2-Forrelation problem provides an optimal separation between classical and quantum query complexity and is also the problem used for separating $\mathsf{BQP}$ and $\mathsf{PH}$ relative to an oracle. A natural question is therefore to ask what are the minimal quantum resources needed to solve this problem. We show that 2-Forrelation can be solved using Instantaneous Quantum Polynomial-time ($\mathsf{IQP}$) circuits, a restricted model of quantum computation in which all gates commute. Concretely, two $\mathsf{IQP}$ circuits with two quantum queries and efficient classical processing suffice. For the signed variant of 2-Forrelation, even a single $\mathsf{IQP}$ circuit and query suffices. This answers a recent open question of Girish (arXiv:2510.06385) on the power of commuting quantum computations. We use this to show that $(\mathsf{BPP}^{\mathsf{IQP}})^O \not\subseteq \mathsf{PH}^O$ relative to an oracle $O$, strengthening the result of Raz and Tal (STOC 2019). Our results show that $\mathsf{IQP}$ circuits can be used for classically hard decision problems, thus providing a new route for showing quantum advantage with $\mathsf{IQP}$ circuits, avoiding the verification difficulties associated with sampling tasks. We also prove Fourier growth bounds for $\mathsf{IQP}$ circuits in terms of the size of their accepting set. The key ingredient is an algebraic identity of the quadratic function $Q(x) = \sum_{i < j} x_ix_j$ that allows extracting inner-product phases within an $\mathsf{IQP}$ circuit.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the 2-Forrelation problem, which provides optimal separation between classical and quantum query complexity, can be solved using IQP (Instantaneous Quantum Polynomial-time) circuits, answering an open question about the power of commuting quantum computations.
AI.motivation: To determine the minimal quantum resources needed to solve the 2-Forrelation problem, which is known for optimal classical-quantum separation and BQP-PH oracle separation, and to address Girish's open question about the power of commuting quantum computations (IQP circuits).
AI.method: Use IQP circuits (where all gates commute) with limited quantum queries and efficient classical processing. For standard 2-Forrelation, two IQP circuits with two quantum queries suffice; for signed variant, a single IQP circuit and query suffices. Key technique involves an algebraic identity of quadratic function Q(x) = Σ_{i<j} x_i x_j to extract inner-product phases within IQP circuits.
AI.result: Proves that (BPP^IQP)^O ⊈ PH^O relative to an oracle O, strengthening Raz and Tal's result. Shows IQP circuits can solve classically hard decision problems, providing a new route for quantum advantage with IQP circuits that avoids verification difficulties of sampling tasks. Also proves Fourier growth bounds for IQP circuits based on size of their accepting set.
AI.conclusion: IQP circuits, despite their commuting gate restriction, are sufficiently powerful to solve the 2-Forrelation problem, answering Girish's open question and demonstrating that commuting quantum computations can achieve quantum advantage for decision problems, offering an alternative to sampling-based quantum advantage demonstrations.

title: General framework for anticoncentration and linear cross-entropy benchmarking in photonic quantum advantage experiments
summary: Photonic architectures are one of the leading platforms for demonstrating quantum computational advantage, with Boson Sampling and Gaussian Boson Sampling as the primary schemes. Yet, we lack for these photonic primitives a systematic theoretical understanding of linear cross-entropy benchmarking (LXEB), which is a central tool for testing quantum advantage proposals. In this work, we develop a representation-theoretic framework for the classical computation of average LXEB scores and second moments of output probability distributions, covering a range of quantum advantage experiments based on scattering $n$-photon states through $m$-mode Haar-random interferometers. Our methods apply in any regime, including the saturated regime, where the (expected) number of photons is comparable to the number of optical modes. The same second-moment techniques also allow us to prove anticoncentration for traditional Fock-state Boson Sampling in the saturated regime. Interestingly, for Gaussian Boson Sampling second moments are not sufficient to establish a meaningful anticoncentration statement. The technical core of our approach rests on decomposing two copies of the $n$-particle bosonic space $\mathrm{Sym}^n(\mathbb{C}^m)$ into irreducible representations of $\mathrm{U}(m)$. This reduces two-copy Haar averages to computing purities of initial states after partial traces over particles, highlighting the role that particle entanglement plays for LXEB and anticoncentration.

AI.tldr: Developed representation-theoretic framework for analyzing linear cross-entropy benchmarking (LXEB) in photonic quantum advantage experiments, enabling classical computation of average LXEB scores and second moments across all regimes including saturated photon/mode regimes.
AI.motivation: Lack systematic theoretical understanding of linear cross-entropy benchmarking (LXEB) for photonic quantum advantage schemes like Boson Sampling and Gaussian Boson Sampling, despite LXEB being central for testing quantum advantage proposals.
AI.method: Representation-theoretic framework decomposing two copies of n-particle bosonic space Symⁿ(ℂᵐ) into irreducible representations of U(m), reducing two-copy Haar averages to computing purities of initial states after partial traces over particles.
AI.result: Enables classical computation of average LXEB scores and second moments across all regimes (including saturated regime where photon count ≈ mode count), proves anticoncentration for Fock-state Boson Sampling in saturated regime, shows second moments insufficient for Gaussian Boson Sampling anticoncentration.
AI.conclusion: Provides systematic theoretical framework for analyzing LXEB in photonic quantum advantage experiments, reveals fundamental differences between Boson Sampling and Gaussian Boson Sampling regarding anticoncentration, highlights role of particle entanglement in LXEB and anticoncentration.

title: Computing the free energy of quantum Coulomb gases and molecules via quantum Gibbs sampling
summary: We develop a quantum algorithm for estimating the free energy as well as the total Gibbs state of interacting quantum Coulomb gases and molecular systems in dimensions $d \in \{2,3\}$ at finite temperature. These systems lie beyond the reach of existing methods due to their singular interactions and infinite-dimensional Hilbert space structure. First, we show that the free energy of the full many-body Hamiltonian can be approximated by that of the same Hamiltonian with a finite-rank low-energy truncation of the interaction, with an explicit error bound polynomial in the particle number. This reduces the problem to a controlled finite-rank perturbation problem. Second, we introduce a quantum Gibbs sampling scheme tailored to this truncated system, based on a class of quantum Markov semigroups. Our main analytical result establishes that the associated generator has a strictly positive spectral gap for every truncation, implying exponential convergence to the target Gibbs state. This provides, to our knowledge, the first rigorous mixing-time guarantee for Gibbs sampling in a Coulomb interacting continuous-variable quantum system. Finally, we give an explicit quantum circuit implementation of the dynamics and derive an end-to-end complexity bound for approximating the free energy and the Gibbs state itself. Our results provide a mathematically rigorous route to quantum algorithms for free energy estimation in interacting quantum systems, without relying on classical approximations such as the Born-Oppenheimer reduction.

AI.tldr: Quantum algorithm for estimating free energy and Gibbs state of interacting quantum Coulomb gases in 2D/3D at finite temperature, overcoming singular interactions and infinite-dimensional Hilbert space challenges.
AI.motivation: Existing methods cannot handle interacting quantum Coulomb gases and molecular systems due to their singular interactions and infinite-dimensional Hilbert space structure, creating a need for new quantum algorithmic approaches.
AI.method: 1) Approximate full Hamiltonian free energy using finite-rank low-energy truncation of interaction with polynomial error bounds; 2) Develop quantum Gibbs sampling scheme using quantum Markov semigroups with guaranteed spectral gap; 3) Provide explicit quantum circuit implementation and complexity analysis.
AI.result: First rigorous mixing-time guarantee for Gibbs sampling in Coulomb interacting continuous-variable quantum systems, with exponential convergence to target Gibbs state and explicit end-to-end complexity bounds for free energy estimation.
AI.conclusion: Provides mathematically rigorous quantum algorithmic route for free energy estimation in interacting quantum systems without relying on classical approximations like Born-Oppenheimer reduction.

title: Cloning is as Hard as Learning for Stabilizer States
summary: The impossibility of simultaneously cloning non-orthogonal states lies at the foundations of quantum theory. Even when allowing for approximation errors, cloning an arbitrary unknown pure state requires as many initial copies as needed to fully learn the state. Rather than arbitrary unknown states, modern quantum learning theory often considers structured classes of states and exploits such structure to develop learning algorithms that outperform general-state tomography. This raises the question: How do the sample complexities of learning and cloning relate for such structured classes? We answer this question for an important class of states. Namely, for $n$-qubit stabilizer states, we show that the optimal sample complexity of cloning is $Θ(n)$. Thus, also for this structured class of states, cloning is as hard as learning. To prove these results, we use representation-theoretic tools in the recently proposed Abelian State Hidden Subgroup framework and a new structured version of the recently introduced random purification channel to relate stabilizer state cloning to a variant of the sample amplification problem for probability distributions that was recently introduced in classical learning theory. This allows us to obtain our cloning lower bounds by proving new sample amplification lower bounds for classes of distributions with an underlying linear structure. Our results provide a more fine-grained perspective on No-Cloning theorems, opening up connections from foundations to quantum learning theory and quantum cryptography.

AI.tldr: For n-qubit stabilizer states, optimal cloning sample complexity is Θ(n), showing cloning is as hard as learning even for structured state classes.
AI.motivation: Traditional no-cloning theorems show cloning arbitrary unknown pure states requires as many copies as learning. This paper investigates whether structured classes of states (like stabilizer states) allow more efficient cloning relative to learning.
AI.method: Uses representation-theoretic tools in Abelian State Hidden Subgroup framework and introduces structured random purification channel to relate stabilizer state cloning to sample amplification for distributions with linear structure.
AI.result: For n-qubit stabilizer states, optimal cloning sample complexity is Θ(n), matching learning complexity. Proves new sample amplification lower bounds for linear-structured distribution classes.
AI.conclusion: Cloning remains as hard as learning even for structured stabilizer states, providing fine-grained perspective on No-Cloning theorems and connecting foundations to quantum learning theory and cryptography.

title: Generation of Schrödinger cat-like states via degenerate dual pump spontaneous four-wave mixing in a $χ^{(3)}$ microring resonator
summary: We theoretically investigate the generation of non-Gaussian quantum states, specifically Schrödinger cat-like states (SCLSs), via degenerate dual-pump spontaneous four-wave mixing in a $χ^{(3)}$-based microring resonator. By introducing a unitary transformation that exactly decouples the self-phase modulation (SPM) and cross-phase modulation (XPM) terms, we reduce the full nonlinear Hamiltonian to an effective three-mode interaction. The resulting dynamics (decoupled and full Hamiltonians) are studied using the Lindblad master equation, accounting for cavity losses. Unlike semiclassical or parametric approximations, our full quantum mechanical approach explicitly includes quantum pump depletion, which enables the emergence and observation of non-Gaussian features. We compute the Wigner function, photon number distributions, quadrature variances, Fano factor, Schmidt number, and fidelity to characterize the generated states. For the non-dissipative case, we find that the signal mode $\hat{b}_3$ or $\hat{a}_3$ exhibits clear non-Gaussian features with a structured Wigner function and even-dominated photon number distribution, characteristic of an even coherent state. In the presence of dissipation ($γ_j = 0.2$), the interference fringes become faint, odd photon numbers appear, and the fidelity with the ideal state remains high ($>0.9$), indicating robustness. The pump mode $\hat{b}_1$ or $\hat{a}_1$ remains Gaussian, while both modes display super-Poissonian statistics and entanglement ($>2$). Our results demonstrate that degenerate dual-pump spontaneous four-wave mixing in microring resonators is a promising platform for generating and controlling cat-like states under dissipative conditions.

AI.tldr: Degenerate dual-pump spontaneous four-wave mixing in χ³ microring resonators can generate Schrödinger cat-like states through quantum pump depletion, with robust non-Gaussian features persisting under dissipation.
AI.motivation: To theoretically investigate generation of non-Gaussian quantum states (Schrödinger cat-like states) via degenerate dual-pump spontaneous four-wave mixing in χ³-based microring resonators, overcoming limitations of semiclassical or parametric approximations by including quantum pump depletion effects.
AI.method: Introduce unitary transformation to decouple SPM and XPM terms, reducing full nonlinear Hamiltonian to effective three-mode interaction. Study dynamics using Lindblad master equation accounting for cavity losses. Compute Wigner function, photon number distributions, quadrature variances, Fano factor, Schmidt number, and fidelity for characterization.
AI.result: Signal mode exhibits clear non-Gaussian features with structured Wigner function and even-dominated photon number distribution (characteristic of even coherent state). Under dissipation (γ_j = 0.2), interference fringes become faint but fidelity remains high (>0.9). Pump mode remains Gaussian, both modes show super-Poissonian statistics and entanglement (>2).
AI.conclusion: Degenerate dual-pump spontaneous four-wave mixing in microring resonators is a promising platform for generating and controlling cat-like states under dissipative conditions, with robust non-Gaussian features maintained despite cavity losses.

title: Super-Constant Weight Dicke States in Constant Depth Without Fanout
summary:
An $n$-qubit Dicke state of weight $k$, is the uniform superposition over all $n$-bit strings of Hamming weight $k$. Dicke states are an entanglement resource with important practical applications in the NISQ era and, for instance, play a central role in Decoded Quantum Interferometry (DQI). Furthermore, any symmetric state can be expressed as a superposition of Dicke states.
  First, we give explicit constant-depth circuits that prepare $n$-qubit Dicke states for all $k \leq \text{polylog}(n)$, using only multi-qubit Toffoli gates and single-qubit unitaries. This gives the first $\text{QAC}^0$ construction of super-constant weight Dicke states. Previous constant-depth constructions for any super-constant $k$ required the FANOUT$_n$ gate, while $\text{QAC}^0$ is only known to implement FANOUT$_k$ for $k$ up to $\text{polylog}(n)$. Moreover, we show that any weight-$k$ Dicke state can be constructed with access to FANOUT$_{\min(k,n-k)}$, rather than FANOUT$_n$. Combined with recent hardness results, this yields a tight characterization: for $k \leq n/2$, weight-$k$ Dicke states can be prepared in $\text{QAC}^0$ if and only if FANOUT$_k \in \text{QAC}^0$.
  We further extend our techniques to show that, in fact, \emph{any} superposition of $n$-qubit Dicke states of weight at most $k$ can be prepared in $\text{QAC}^0$ with access to FANOUT$_k$. Taking $k = n$, we obtain the first $O(1)$-depth unitary construction for arbitrary symmetric states. In particular, any symmetric state can be prepared in constant depth on quantum hardware architectures that support FANOUT$_n$, such as trapped ions with native global entangling operations.

AI.tldr: Constant-depth quantum circuits for preparing Dicke states and arbitrary symmetric states using FANOUT gates, establishing tight complexity characterizations for QAC⁰ implementation.
AI.motivation: Dicke states are important entanglement resources with practical applications in NISQ-era quantum computing, particularly in Decoded Quantum Interferometry. They serve as a basis for all symmetric states, making efficient preparation crucial for quantum algorithms.
AI.method: Develop explicit constant-depth circuits using multi-qubit Toffoli gates and single-qubit unitaries. Show that weight-k Dicke states can be constructed with access to FANOUT_min(k,n-k) rather than FANOUT_n. Extend techniques to prepare any superposition of Dicke states with weight at most k using FANOUT_k.
AI.result: First QAC⁰ construction of super-constant weight Dicke states for k ≤ polylog(n). Tight characterization: for k ≤ n/2, weight-k Dicke states can be prepared in QAC⁰ if and only if FANOUT_k ∈ QAC⁰. First O(1)-depth unitary construction for arbitrary symmetric states using FANOUT_n.
AI.conclusion: The work provides efficient constant-depth quantum circuits for preparing Dicke states and arbitrary symmetric states, establishing fundamental complexity-theoretic relationships between Dicke state preparation and FANOUT gate capabilities in QAC⁰ circuits.

title: Ensembles of random quantum states tunable from volume law to area law
summary: A standard approach to generate random pure quantum states relies on sampling from the Haar measure. However, the entanglement properties of such states present a fundamental challenge for their general applicability. Here, we introduce the $σ$-ensembles $\unicode{x2013}$ a family of random quantum states with only a single control parameter. Crucially, these states are designed such that they can be tuned between volume-law and area-law behavior, which has been a major obstacle thus far. We construct representatives of this ensemble by imposing a probability distribution on the eigenvalues of the successive subsystems, and subsequently reconstructing a compatible global state using the matrix product state (MPS) formalism. Due to their area-law entanglement, our approach circumvents the intractability of Haar-random pure states in classical simulations of quantum systems and is more representative of typical Hamiltonian ground states.

AI.tldr: Introduces σ-ensembles - a family of random quantum states with a single control parameter that can be tuned between volume-law and area-law entanglement scaling, addressing the limitations of Haar-random states for classical simulations.
AI.motivation: Haar-random pure states have entanglement properties that make them intractable for classical simulations and not representative of typical Hamiltonian ground states, which often exhibit area-law entanglement.
AI.method: Construct σ-ensembles by imposing a probability distribution on eigenvalues of successive subsystems, then reconstruct compatible global states using matrix product state (MPS) formalism.
AI.result: Creates tunable random quantum states that can exhibit either volume-law or area-law entanglement scaling through a single control parameter, making them more suitable for classical simulations.
AI.conclusion: σ-ensembles provide a practical alternative to Haar-random states for studying quantum systems, particularly for classical simulations where area-law entanglement is essential for tractability and better represents typical ground states.

title: Heuristic Search for Minimum-Distance Upper-Bound Witnesses in Quantum APM-LDPC Codes
summary: This paper investigates certified upper bounds on the minimum distance of an explicit family of Calderbank-Shor-Steane quantum LDPC codes constructed from affine permutation matrices. All codes considered here have active Tanner graphs of girth eight. Rather than attempting to prove a general lower bound for the full code distance, we focus on constructing low-weight non-stabilizer logical representatives, which yield valid upper bounds once they are verified to lie in the opposite parity-check kernel and outside the stabilizer row space. We develop a unified framework for such witnesses arising from latent row relations, restricted-lift subspaces including block-compressed, selected-fiber, and CRT-stripe constructions, cycle- 8 elementary trapping-set structures, and decoder-failure residuals. In every case, search is used only to generate candidates; the reported bounds begin only after explicit kernel and row-space exclusion tests have been passed. For the latent part, we also identify a block-compression criterion under which the certification becomes exact. Applying these methods to representative APM-LDPC codes sharpens previously reported upper bounds and provides concrete certified values across the explored parameter range.

AI.tldr: This paper develops methods to compute certified upper bounds on the minimum distance of affine permutation matrix (APM) quantum LDPC codes by constructing low-weight logical operators and verifying they are valid non-stabilizer representatives.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide rigorous upper bounds on the minimum distance of explicit APM quantum LDPC codes, which have Tanner graphs of girth eight. Rather than attempting general lower bounds, the focus is on constructing concrete certified upper bounds through verified low-weight logical operators.
AI.method: The authors develop a unified framework for constructing low-weight logical representatives using: 1) latent row relations, 2) restricted-lift subspaces (block-compressed, selected-fiber, and CRT-stripe constructions), 3) cycle-8 elementary trapping-set structures, and 4) decoder-failure residuals. Search generates candidates, but bounds are only reported after explicit verification that they lie in the opposite parity-check kernel and outside the stabilizer row space.
AI.result: The methods are applied to representative APM-LDPC codes, sharpening previously reported upper bounds and providing concrete certified values across the explored parameter range. For the latent part, a block-compression criterion is identified under which certification becomes exact.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a systematic approach for computing certified upper bounds on quantum code distances through verified construction of low-weight logical operators, with applications to APM quantum LDPC codes that improve existing bounds.
